executive_summary: USD/JPY closed at 156.38 on 6 May 2026, down from the 160.74 wartime peak hit on 30 April after suspected Ministry of Finance intervention of approximately ¥5.4 trillion (~$34.5bn) on 1 May. The yen sits at the centre of a 'stagflation triangle': Brent at ~$102/bbl (down from a $120 wartime peak but still ~35% above pre-war levels) is wrecking Japan's terms of trade, while a 6-3 hawkish hold at the 28 April BOJ MPM (rate held at 0.75%) showed three Policy Board members dissenting in favour of an immediate 25bp hike to 1.00% — the most fractured BOJ vote since 2016. The April Outlook Report raised the FY2026 core CPI projection to 2.8% (from 1.9% in January) and cut FY2026 GDP to 0.5% (from 1.0%), an explicit stagflationary recalibration. The US-Japan 10Y spread has compressed to ~187bp (US 10Y 4.36% vs JGB 10Y 2.50%, the highest since July 1997), structurally narrowing the dominant USD/JPY driver. CFTC positioning shows leveraged funds net long JPY ~+56,400 contracts in the week ending 28 April — an atypical configuration after years of structural shorts, removing the short-squeeze catalyst while reducing intervention-failure tail risk. Performance: 1M -2.51% (yen stronger), 3M +1.4% (yen weaker), 6M +1.0% (yen weaker), 1Y +8.20% (yen materially weaker). Headline tactical stance is Bullish JPY with conviction 3/5 — trade the 155-160 range with intervention asymmetry capping the topside. Strategic stance is Bullish JPY with conviction 3/5 — target 150-152 over 1-6 months on probable June or July BOJ hike, Fed easing path, and Iran ceasefire-driven oil normalisation toward $80, with Bloomberg consensus median at 153 by end-2026.

market_context.recent_regime: The dominant narrative in early May is a fragile two-handed regime: Iran war de-escalation hopes (Trump declared Operation Epic Fury 'concluded' 5 May; Project Freedom paused 6 May to allow Tehran framework talks) are pulling oil lower and the yen stronger, while BOJ caution and Fed dovish pricing keep the directional asymmetry from collapsing into a one-way move. Volatility regimes have reset higher than the pre-war low-vol grind — Nikkei VI ~29.5 reflects ongoing equity stress despite the SPX printing record highs at 7,259 (5 May). The May 1 intervention was the largest single-shot defence of yen since July 2024's $36.8bn episode, establishing a credible 160-zone topside. The cross-currency relative-value picture has CHF outperforming JPY as the cleaner safe-haven during this oil-shock episode, a regime feature that reverses if the BOJ delivers a June hike.

tactical.analysis: The BOJ delivered a hawkish hold on 28 April 2026 with a 6-3 vote — Nakagawa, Takata and Tamura all dissented in favour of an immediate hike to 1.00%. This is the most fractured BOJ vote since 2016 and the first three-way hawkish dissent under Governor Ueda. The April Outlook Report raised FY2026 core CPI to 2.8% (from 1.9% in January) and explicitly characterised inflation as broadly consistent with the 2% target through FY2027. The BOJ policy rate at 0.75% ranks lowest in G10+SGD by a wide margin — 275-300bp below Fed funds, 125bp below ECB deposit, 360bp below RBA cash. However, the relevant tactical question is the change in the differential: US-Japan 10Y spread compressed to ~186bp (US 10Y 4.36% vs JGB 10Y 2.50%) — the narrowest in years and a clear yen-supportive structural shift. Three dissenters at the April meeting plus Ueda's explicit reference to 'real interest rates significantly low' make a June 16-17 hike a live (~30-40% implied) risk that markets are not fully pricing. OIS-implied path sees ~10bp of hikes by July (i.e., partial pricing of one move). Carry-to-vol has compressed materially as JPY vol stays elevated and rate gaps narrow.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing a slow, two-hike-per-year BOJ normalisation path with the next hike in July or September. Three Board members dissenting at April creates an asymmetric path: a June hike would likely shock USD/JPY 200-300 pips lower, while a hold is already the consensus base case. The edge is owning yen upside (or USD/JPY downside) into June MPM at compressed implieds.

strategic.analysis: Strategic horizon allows the policy-divergence narrative to play out fully. MUFG's house view sees BOJ at 1.00% by Q2/Q3 2026 and 1.25% by Q4 2026 / Q1 2027. Sony Financial sees terminal at 1.25% by end-2026. With three dissenters already at 1.00%, the bar to a June or July hike is low. The Fed at 3.50-3.75% retains a dovish bias with markets pricing ~25bp of cuts by end-2026; this is an asymmetric path narrowing. By Q4 2026, the implied US-Japan policy-rate gap could compress from ~285bp today to ~225bp — a 60bp narrowing that historically translates to USD/JPY moving 6-10 figures lower. The BOJ JGB taper has stepped down from ¥400bn/quarter to ¥200bn/quarter effective April 2026 — a calibrated pace that reduces BOP risk while keeping front-end normalisation viable. Strategic carry framework: even at projected 1.00% BOJ, JPY carry remains structurally negative vs USD, EUR, GBP, AUD, NZD, CAD; the funding-currency status persists but at compressed magnitude. Importantly, the strategic asymmetry favours yen because Fed cuts are more elastic (cyclical) than BOJ hikes (structural normalisation) — Fed dovishness can compress the gap quickly, while BOJ hawkishness compresses it slowly but durably.

tactical.analysis: Japan's national core CPI (ex-fresh food) accelerated to +1.8% YoY in March (from 1.6% in February) — the first acceleration in four months. The core-core measure (ex-fresh food and energy) at +2.4% stays stickily above target. Headline CPI at +1.5% is dragged down by fuel subsidies (electricity -8.0%, gas -5.2% YoY in March). Tokyo April core CPI eased to +1.5%. Q4 2025 GDP printed at +0.3% QoQ (1.2% annualised), revised up from preliminary. Q1 2026 GDP preliminary releases 19 May — Polymarket implies 38% probability of 0.9-1.1% YoY. The April Tankan was solid pre-war: large manufacturers DI +17 (from +15), large non-manufacturers +36 — both showing forward-3M deterioration on Iran-war concerns. April flash composite PMI 52.4 (mfg surged to 54.9, services slumped to 51.2) with business confidence at the weakest since August 2020. Unemployment at 2.7% with a tight 1.18 jobs-to-applicants ratio. Shunto 2026 delivered a 5.09% aggregate wage hike in RENGO's third tally (3 April) — the third consecutive year above 5%. The wage-inflation virtuous cycle remains intact, supporting BOJ normalisation momentum and yen.

strategic.analysis: The BOJ's April Outlook Report explicitly raised FY2026 core CPI to 2.8% (from 1.9% in January) and FY2027 to 2.3% — these are the highest forecasts since the BOJ began publishing the price stability target. The Bank's own statement says 'underlying inflation is likely to be at a level broadly consistent with its 2% target in the second half of fiscal 2026 and through fiscal 2027' — central-bank prose for 'we should be hiking.' Strategic growth: FY2026 GDP cut to 0.5% (from 1.0%), reflecting Iran-war oil shock. This stagflation mix forces BOJ to focus on inflation given anchored expectations. Shunto 2026 at 5.09% is slightly below 2025's 5.25% RENGO final tally but base-pay growth of ~3.85% (first-tally measure) supports services-inflation persistence. The structural growth trend remains weak (potential GDP ~0.5-1%) due to demographics — but the inflation impulse from imported energy plus sustained wages is independent of cyclical growth. Citi Economic Surprise Index NOT FOUND for current Japan reading. Strategic risk: if Iran ceasefire holds and Brent reverts to $80, energy subsidies will be unwound and price levels normalise — but the wage cycle locked in via Shunto is a 12M effect that anchors a higher inflation floor than pre-war.

factors.block: Factor 5: External Balance & Terms of Trade

tactical.analysis: The Iran-war energy shock has materially deteriorated Japan's terms of trade. Brent at ~$102 is up roughly 35% from pre-war ~$75 levels even after the May 6 ceasefire-driven plunge. Japan imports approximately 95.9% of its crude oil from the Middle East in FY2024 — the highest level since FY1965 per IEEJ/METI data. The trade balance is feeling the strain: March 2026 trade balance came in at ¥+667.0bn (vs ¥+1,106bn expected), with imports +10.9% YoY (fastest since Jan 2025) outpacing the +11.7% export gain. JKM LNG: NOT FOUND specifically for May 2026 but elevated alongside Brent given Strait of Hormuz disruption. Japan's emergency oil reserves drawdown of 80 million barrels (45 days of demand) since 16 March is mitigating panic but not solving the import-cost issue. Tactically, the next trade balance release (mid-May) will show April data, with Iran-war prices fully embedded — risk skews to negative trade-balance surprise. The current account remains in surplus despite trade strain: February current account at ¥+3,932.7bn (largest since September), with primary income of ¥+4,240.3bn providing a structural cushion. But the goods-balance compression (¥+267.6bn in February vs ¥+810bn YoY) is the tactical signal — yen-bearish.
key_data_points: Japan ME crude oil dependence FY2024: 95.9% (highest since 1965)
Emergency oil reserves released since 16 Mar: 80mn barrels
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing the Iran ceasefire as a partial resolution that will allow Brent to revert toward $90 over coming weeks — implying gradual TOT improvement. Risk: if the ceasefire fails (Trump warned May 6 'If they don't agree, the bombing starts'), Brent re-spikes to $120+ and Japan's terms of trade re-deteriorate sharply.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, Japan's external position is resilient because the primary income surplus structurally dominates the trade balance. The +$3.3T Net International Investment Position (largest in the world) generates persistent income flows that buffer trade shocks. Trading partner concentration: China is the largest trade partner (~22% combined exports+imports) followed by US (~16%) and ASEAN. Trade-policy risks: Trump tariff posture and US-Japan trade negotiations are an open issue but not currently active. Reserve currency dynamics (IMF COFER): JPY's share in global reserves is ~5-6%, slightly below the 5Y average — modest, marginal yen-supportive structural factor. Capital flow dynamics: Japanese institutional foreign-bond purchases (lifers, banks) at ~¥4-5T quarterly in normal times; during USD/JPY weakness episodes (sub-150), purchases accelerate; during weakness >155, purchases pause as hedging costs bite. Strategic terms of trade outlook: if Iran ceasefire holds and Brent reverts to $80 by Q3, Japan's TOT would meaningfully improve from current levels, restoring yen support. If ceasefire fails, Brent at $120 sustainably would create a structural drag through 2027. The S&P Global piece on 'Japanese refiners recognize need to reduce 95% Middle East crude dependency' flags the strategic geopolitical risk.

tactical.analysis: The 28 April 2026 BOJ meeting was the most hawkish hold in Ueda's tenure. Three Policy Board members (Nakagawa, Takata, Tamura) dissented in favour of an immediate 25bp hike to 1.00% — the most fractured BOJ vote since the 2016 introduction of negative rates. ZeroHedge characterised it as 'the biggest divergence of opinion under governor Kazuo Ueda, and since the launch of the bank's negative interest rate policy in 2016'. Ueda's press conference stated explicitly: 'Need to pay close attention to how Middle East developments affect financial, FX markets… BoJ will continue to raise policy rate, adjust degree of monetary accommodation according to economic activity, prices, financial conditions… Real interest rates are at significantly low levels'. The 'real interest rates significantly low' phrase is central-bank prose for a hawkish bias — Ueda is preparing the market for a near-term hike. The Outlook Report's FY2026 core CPI revision to 2.8% is the largest single-meeting upward revision in the BOJ's history of the price-stability target. The credibility issue: BOJ communication has been historically opaque (YCC tweaks 2022, YCC end 2024 both surprised). Ueda's communication has been more transparent. The market trusts the BOJ to follow through on hawkish guidance more than it once did.

strategic.analysis: Strategically, the BOJ guidance framework has decisively shifted toward a normalisation bias. The 2.8% FY2026 / 2.3% FY2027 / 2.0% FY2028 core CPI path is fully consistent with sustained policy-rate increases through 2027. Deputy Governor Himino is voting for hold but has not signalled hard dovish bias. Communication style: Ueda has been more transparent than predecessors; the market increasingly trusts BOJ guidance. Upcoming communication events: Summary of Opinions (~7 May), individual board member speeches (Tamura speech ~mid-May), BOJ Outlook on schedule (next October). The credibility test: if BOJ delivers June or July hike with 5-4 or tighter vote, market re-prices the entire 2026-27 path higher and yen rallies meaningfully.

tactical.analysis: Japan's sovereign credit profile is stable but structurally concerning. S&P A+ / Moody's A1 / Fitch A (all stable outlook). Debt-to-GDP at ~250% gross is the highest in the developed world. The unique feature: ~50% of JGBs are held by the BOJ, creating a circular fiscal-monetary dynamic that has historically suppressed yields and refinancing risk. Tactical fiscal-risk indicators: 30Y JGB at ~3.85% / 40Y at 4.21% reached January 2026 record-area levels, reflecting Takaichi fiscal stimulus pricing. The Wolf Street commentary 'JGB 10Y highest since 1997 at 2.50%, but bond vigilantes still dead' captures the dynamic — yields rising but BOJ remains the dominant buyer absorbing supply. Sovereign CDS: 5Y JCDS at NOT FOUND specific level but historically low (sub-30bp). Fiscal stimulus commitments: Takaichi's ¥21.3T supplementary stimulus package (Nov 2025) is being implemented; FY2026 ¥120T initial budget approved Dec 26, 2025. PM stability: high. Tactical fiscal-risk impact on JPY: modest — the JGB yield rise is supportive of yen via rate-differential channel, but the supply-side stimulus is yen-bearish via fiscal leverage concerns.

strategic.analysis: Strategically, Japan's fiscal trajectory is a slow-burn risk that becomes material if BOJ normalisation accelerates beyond market pricing. Each 100bp rise in average JGB yield costs ~2% of GDP in additional debt servicing — a meaningful fiscal headwind that could limit BOJ hawkishness. Takaichi's fiscal stimulus orientation (consumption-tax suspension on food, AI/semiconductor spending) creates ongoing supply pressure on JGBs. The 30Y/40Y JGB yields at multi-decade highs reflect this dynamic. Fitch revised Japan outlook from negative to stable in March 2022; subsequent annual reviews have maintained the stable outlook. Sovereign CDS spread trend: stable, no recent activity. Strategic risk scenario: if BOJ delivers two hikes by Q3 2026 and the 10Y JGB breaks 3% in late 2026, the fiscal-debt dynamic becomes a market-relevant constraint and BOJ may face implicit pressure to slow normalisation — JPY-bearish second-order effect. The 'Takaichi trade' narrative (stimulus + structural growth) has been associated with higher long-end yields and yen weakness historically — currently quiescent.

tactical.rationale: The tactical case for JPY rests on a hawkish BOJ pivot meeting compressing US-Japan rate differentials at a moment when historic short-positioning has already been worked off. The 28 April BOJ vote (6-3 hawkish) is the decisive recent catalyst — three Policy Board members dissenting in favour of an immediate hike to 1.00% creates an asymmetric June outcome where a hike would shock USD/JPY 200-300 pips lower while a hold is already priced. Inflation & Growth dynamics reinforce this: core CPI accelerating to 1.8% (March) with core-core at 2.4% and the BOJ raising FY2026 core CPI to 2.8% embed a normalisation pathway that the market has not fully repriced. The market expects ~10bp of June hike pricing; we expect ~30-40% probability of an actual move. The compressed US-Japan 10Y spread to 186bp is the cleanest tactical USD/JPY driver, narrowing further if US 10Y eases on Fed dovish bias or JGB 10Y rises on Takaichi stimulus pricing. Risk Appetite is genuinely Neutral — JPY's safe-haven character is partially compromised in this oil-shock regime, with CHF outperforming as the cleaner safe-haven — so the tactical view doesn't depend on global risk-off mechanics. Flows & Positioning is Neutral because the historic short-squeeze catalyst has been worked off (leveraged funds +56k net long), which both reduces upside fuel and reduces a key risk to a 162+ rally. The single bearish factor — External Balance — reflects Brent at $102 still ~35% above pre-war levels, hurting Japan's terms of trade, but its 0.06 tactical weight contains the impact. The path of least resistance is sideways-to-down: USD/JPY drifts in a 155-160 corridor with intervention-defined topside and rate-differential-supported downside, with potential for sharper move lower if June BOJ surprises or Iran ceasefire firms decisively. Conviction drivers: the 6-3 BOJ vote is unprecedented under Ueda, real-rates language is explicit, Bloomberg consensus median (153) aligns with directional bias though we expect slightly more aggressive yen rally on hawkish surprise. Actionability: tradable through 1M USD/JPY puts at compressed implieds, short USD/JPY spot with 161 stops, or short AUD/JPY spot if seeking risk-aware expression.

strategic.rationale: The strategic case for JPY combines a multi-meeting BOJ normalisation path with structural mean-reversion forces from extreme PPP undervaluation. Policy & Rates remains the dominant factor with 70 confidence — MUFG sees BOJ at 1.00% by Q2/Q3 and 1.25% by Q4 2026 while market pricing implies less than that, creating ~50bp of strategic mispricing. The Fed's dovish bias and ~25bp of cuts priced for H2 2026 compress the differential from a different angle. Combined, the projected end-2026 US-Japan policy gap of ~225bp (vs ~285bp now) implies USD/JPY toward 148-152, fully consistent with Bloomberg consensus (153). Guidance & Credibility is a strategic anchor at 70 confidence — the BOJ Outlook Report's FY2026 core CPI revision to 2.8% is the largest single-meeting upward revision since the price-stability target was adopted, signalling a multi-quarter normalisation path. Inflation & Growth dynamics support: Shunto at 5.09% anchors a wage-cycle floor that prevents inflation reverting to <1% even if energy normalises, locking in BOJ rate-rise momentum. USD Liquidity & Funding contributes structurally — at compressed differentials, the JPY funding-currency role weakens, removing a multi-year structural headwind. External Balance shifts to Neutral over the strategic horizon because the +$3.3T NIIP and primary income surplus dominate over the cyclical trade-balance damage from oil. Risk Appetite shifts to Bullish JPY (50 confidence) over 1-6M as the energy-import overlay fades and the classical safe-haven repatriation channel reactivates. The path of least resistance is a phased move: 156 → 153-155 in May-June (BOJ MPM event), 150-153 in Q3 (post-July hike), 148-152 by Q4 (Fed cut + second BOJ hike). Conviction drivers: the BOJ has explicitly told the market it will hike, has three voters already at 1.00%, and faces structural inflation pressure — this is more than a coin flip, but conviction is capped at 3/5 by Iran tail-risk and Trump trade-policy unpredictability. Actionability: highly tradeable through 3M and 6M USD/JPY put structures, long JPY vs short AUD/CAD/NZD (rate-cutting peers), and selectively short EUR/JPY at 184+ if BOJ June surprises hawkish.

JPY rises to rank 1 if BOJ delivers two hikes by Q3.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Tactical and strategic stances are aligned (both Bullish JPY at conviction 3/5). Strategic confidence (62%) slightly exceeds tactical (60%) because the strategic horizon allows the multi-meeting BOJ normalisation path to play out fully and dampens single-event binary risk.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The thesis strengthens over time as the BOJ delivers (or doesn't deliver) the expected hikes — the strategic 1-6M window encompasses 2-3 BOJ MPMs, providing more events for the hawkish-pivot thesis to materialise vs the single-MPM tactical window. External Balance shifts from tactical Bearish to strategic Neutral as primary income surplus dominates over 1-6M.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: The 17 June 2026 BOJ MPM is the dominant single catalyst
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: A BOJ hike with 5-4 or tighter vote converts both horizons to conviction 4/5 and target 148-150 by Q3; a dovish hold preserves the 3/5 tactical conviction but raises strategic uncertainty
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Tactical positioning should be sized for the binary June outcome with stop discipline; strategic positioning can layer on Phase 2 entries (post-June MPM) at better levels regardless of outcome — if dovish hold, strategic short USD/JPY at 158-160 with 162 stop; if hawkish hike, add at 152-154 on retest with 156 stop.

1m.annualised_pct: -2.8% (negative = USD depreciating in forwards = JPY appreciating)

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: The 6M edge of ~280 pips (~1.8%) is the most actionable — short USD/JPY 6M outright captures the gap between forwards and our expected path, with limited downside if consensus is correct (forward roll-down provides natural cushion). The forward curve already prices yen appreciation given the positive US-Japan rate gap, but our directional view extends the move beyond forwards-implied.

key_catalysts.rationale: Norges decision reflects oil-price sensitivity; Riksbank reflects European stagflation. JPY impact via cross-rate convergence flows; secondary impact at most.

First full-month BOP data reflecting late-March/April Iran-war energy import surge. A weaker-than-expected current account confirms terms-of-trade damage and validates the bearish External Balance tactical stance.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — markets expect deterioration; degree uncertain

key_catalysts.rationale: First GDP read covering Iran-war period. Weak growth could signal BOJ caution; strong growth supports hawkish hold thesis. Polymarket implies 38% probability of 0.9-1.1% YoY range.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — Polymarket implies 38% probability of 0.9-1.1% YoY

key_catalysts.rationale: Tokyo CPI is the leading indicator for national CPI. A reading above 2.0% accelerates June MPM hawkish probability and pressures BOJ to deliver June hike. Consensus expects ~1.5-1.7%; upside risk if energy subsidies wound down.
BOC at 2.25% with hold expected; surprise cut would weaken CAD vs JPY via cross-rate. Direct JPY impact minimal.
ECB at 2.00% deposit; market pricing terminal cuts. A hawkish hold preserves euro vs yen via cross-rate. Direct JPY impact minimal.

Three dissenters at April creates ~40-50% probability of June hike. Hike with 5-4 vote would shock USD/JPY 200-300 pips lower; hold with same dissent count maintains current trajectory; dovish hold (8-1) would weaken JPY. Single most important catalyst over the strategic horizon.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — markets price ~35-40% probability of hike

key_catalysts.rationale: Fed expected to hold; market pricing ~25bp cut by year-end. Dovish lean compresses US-Japan rate gap further. Hawkish surprise would re-widen and reverse JPY rally.
Cluster of G10 decisions creates broad cross-currency repricing; SNB likely intervention rhetoric vs CHF strength remains key for JPY safe-haven relative-value. Direct JPY impact via crosses.
Routine BOC decision; CAD/JPY cross-impact only. Direct JPY impact minimal.

Quarterly Outlook Report update. If June was a hold, July becomes the dominant hike candidate. Outlook revisions on FY2026/27 CPI will signal further normalisation path. Combined June OR July hike probability ~70%.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — market expects either June OR July hike (binary aggregate ~70%)

key_catalysts.rationale: First full-quarter GDP fully embedded with Iran-war effects. Weak growth could trigger BOJ caution and slow normalisation pace; strong growth supports continued hike trajectory.
H2 Fed cut probability concentrated around September meeting per OIS pricing. Cut would compress US-Japan rate gap further and accelerate yen rally.
After likely June or July hike, September is candidate for follow-up move depending on data. Second hike would push BOJ rate to 1.25% and deliver further yen support.
Second potential Fed cut window in 2026. If delivered, would compress rate gap further and support yen.
Quarterly Outlook Report; potential for second hike if June delivered. Q4 Outlook update typically signals direction for following fiscal year.

invalidators.condition: Iran ceasefire fails and Brent breaks $130 sustained for 5+ trading days
invalidators.mechanism: Japan's terms of trade collapses (95.9% ME oil dependence), the BOJ would prioritise growth over inflation, and intervention dry powder erodes. Energy-shock dominates BOJ-rate channel.

invalidators.condition: BOJ June 17 MPM delivers dovish hold (8-1 vote, no escalation of dissent) AND Ueda explicitly removes 'real rates significantly low' language
invalidators.mechanism: The hawkish-pivot thesis is invalidated; market repricing shifts BOJ normalisation path further out, narrowing the rate-convergence dynamic. Yen reverts to range-trading.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BOJ statement language and vote split
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: 8-1 or wider hold vote AND removal of 'real rates significantly low' from statement

invalidators.condition: US 10Y Treasury breaks 4.7% sustained for 10+ trading days on Fed hawkish re-pricing
invalidators.mechanism: The US-Japan 10Y spread re-widens past 220bp, restoring USD/JPY upside via rate channel. Carry-trade attractiveness rebuilds.

invalidators.condition: CFTC TFF leveraged funds net long JPY exceeds +100,000 contracts AND USD/JPY fails to break 152
invalidators.mechanism: Positioning becomes a contra-indicator and a squeeze becomes the dominant near-term risk. Long-yen positioning unwind would trigger 4-7 figure USD/JPY rally.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: Lev funds net long >+100,000 contracts AND spot fails 152 floor

invalidators.condition: Tokyo or national core CPI breaks below 1.5% YoY for two consecutive months
invalidators.mechanism: BOJ normalisation pace decelerates and the 2.8% FY2026 projection becomes obviously stale. The wage-inflation virtuous cycle weakens.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: Core CPI <1.5% YoY for 2 consecutive months

invalidators.condition: MoF intervention exceeds combined ¥10T (~$65bn) across multiple rounds without USD/JPY decisively breaking 152
invalidators.mechanism: Intervention exhaustion would signal MoF reserves vs market positioning is losing battle, mirroring 2022 dynamic. Loss of credibility opens door to renewed JPY weakness.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: MoF intervention disclosures, BOJ current-account data

invalidators.condition: Trump announces auto tariffs on Japanese imports (10%+ tariff)
invalidators.mechanism: Japan growth shock + trade war risk would force BOJ caution and potentially reverse normalisation. Yen weakens on growth-shock and BOJ-pause dynamics.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Trump administration trade announcements, USTR section 232/301 publications
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: 10%+ tariff on Japanese auto imports announced

Iran ceasefire emergence reverses the energy-import overlay over the strategic horizon; structural primary income surplus dominates.
JGB curve has stabilised after 30Y/40Y record-area peaks in January; BOJ remains dominant buyer absorbing supply; fiscal stress not currently material to tactical horizon.
May 1 intervention defines a 160 ceiling that constrains directional setup; range-bound regime makes structure factor neutral rather than directional.
Iran ceasefire emergence shifts event-risk balance toward yen-supportive outcomes; framework deal trajectory provides upside catalyst.

signposts_triggered.implication: Major structural shift that REMOVES the prior dossier's positioning bullish-tilt; future yen rallies will be slower but more durable.

conviction_change.explanation: Tactical conviction unchanged at 3/5 (essentially unchanged underlying score: +0.357 → +0.360). Strategic conviction recovered from 2/5 to 3/5 as the Iran-war shock that collapsed strategic conviction in the April dossier (from 4/5 to 2/5) has partially reversed with the ceasefire emergence. Strategic score materially strengthened from +0.204 to +0.408 (+0.20).

new_catalysts: June 16-17 BOJ MPM elevated to CRITICAL status given the three-way April dissent
Iran ceasefire framework deal trajectory (next 2-4 weeks binary)
Suspected May 1 intervention establishes a credible 160 ceiling for tactical price action
BOJ Summary of Opinions release (~7 May) flagged as near-term signpost

seasonality.pattern: Within the 1-6 month strategic horizon, two relevant Japan-specific seasonal patterns exist: mid-year fiscal pressure (September 30 = H1 fiscal close) when Japanese institutions including lifers and corporates conduct mid-year hedging adjustments, historically modest yen-supportive flow in late September–early October; and year-end USD funding stress (November-December) as global USD funding traditionally tightens with banks reducing balance-sheet exposure, modest yen-supportive at year-end via basis dynamics.
seasonality.relevance_window: Both patterns fall within the May-November strategic horizon
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Mid-year H1 close: ~50-100 pip yen-supportive bias historically; Year-end: ~30-80 pip JPY basis impact
seasonality.current_alignment: Neither seasonal pattern dominates the May-November window — the dominant flows are event-driven (BOJ MPMs, Fed FOMCs, Iran trajectory) rather than calendar-driven. Japanese fiscal year-end repatriation (March 31) is already passed and provides no further tactical tailwind.

assumptions: USD/JPY 1M, 3M, 6M implied volatility precise readings NOT FOUND from public sources at search time. Relied on JPY VIX index (MacroMicro) and narrative reports for directional vol assessment. Estimated 1M IV at 9-10% based on May 1 intervention reset and post-event compression.
25-delta risk reversals (1M, 3M) NOT FOUND specifically; characterised based on narrative reports indicating shift toward JPY-call premium post-intervention.
3M JPY/USD cross-currency basis swap level NOT FOUND specifically for May 2026; structurally negative based on long-run norm (~-30 to -50bp historical).

BIS REER (April 2026) reading NOT FOUND for latest precise reading; relied on long-run characterisation that JPY REER sits in bottom decile of post-1990 history. PPP fair value uses OECD PPP rate of 94.65 JPY/USD as base anchor.
2Y, 5Y, 20Y JGB yields for May 6-7 NOT FOUND precisely; estimated based on 10Y JGB at 2.50% and recent curve shape (2s10s ~+145bp).
USD/JPY forward points estimated based on US-Japan rate differential (~285bp gap × forward tenor); precise forward points not directly retrieved from market data.
G10 macro dashboard: several G10 fields (NOK, SEK GDP/CPI/unemployment) flagged NOT FOUND where specific recent readings could not be retrieved; populated with estimates from broader macro context.

Intervention size estimate: adopted Bloomberg's ¥5.4T / $34.5bn figure as primary; the slightly higher CNBC/Reuters ¥5.48T / $35bn figure is a separate inference from the same BOJ current-account data and is within tolerance.
OIS-implied BOJ path: estimated June MPM hike probability of 35-40% based on three-way April dissent and Ueda's hawkish language; precise OIS swap-rate readings not retrieved.

Bloomberg consensus forecast: used the May 1 MUFG-compiled aggregate (153 by end-2026, 152 by Q1 2027) as primary consensus benchmark. JPMorgan's 164 outlier and Nomura's 140-145 (pre-war Goto) noted as range markers.

Iran-US war ('Operation Epic Fury') context: war onset February 28, 2026; framework deal talks begin May 5-6; Strait of Hormuz closed since March 4, 2026 with partial commercial transit during isolated windows; Brent trajectory assumed to revert toward $80-90 if ceasefire holds, risk to $130+ if fails.

Bilateral cross-rate derivations: AUD/JPY, NZD/JPY, CAD/JPY, CHF/JPY, NOK/JPY, SEK/JPY, SGD/JPY computed from USD/JPY 156.38 and prevailing USD/X rates on May 6 (Yahoo Finance composite). Direct JPY cross-rate readings sourced where available (EUR/JPY 183.6, GBP/JPY 213.2 from Yahoo Finance recent quotes).
